text:
TSMC의 ‘AI 3층 케이크’ 개념

Compute(논리 연산)
3D Integration(3D 적층)
Photonics(광학 전송)
embedded_text:
이 테마 안에서 뺑뺑이 돈단 얘기

-
NVIDIA의 젠슨 황 CEO가 최근 데이터센터의 ‘5층 케이크(Five-layer Cake)’ 이론을 제시한 데 이어, TSMC도 ‘AI 3층 케이크’ 개념을 발표. TSMC는 앞으로 AI 칩이 Compute(논리 연산), 3D Integration(3D 적층), Photonics(광학 전송) 등 세 가지 방향의 통합

text:
From Physics classrooms in Nigeria 🇳🇬 to the semiconductor industry in the USA 🇺🇸… still feels surreal sometimes.

I’ll be speaking at the UNESCO International Day of Light 2026 organized by Photonics Nigeria on:

🔬 The Role of Precision Optics in Semiconductor Manufacturing

If you’re curious about semiconductors, photonics, optics, engineering careers, or the future of technology, this session is for you.

📅 May 16, 2026
⏰ 3:00 PM – 5:00 PM WAT
🌐 Online Event

Make sure to help share/retweet so it reach more STEM students! 

See you there! 🚀✨ #Photonics #Semiconductors #Optics #Engineering #Nigeria #ASML #STEM

€27 = current Montega target €30–35 = basic turnaround/SOTP rerate
€40–50 = photonics/semi narrative gets priced
€60+ = strong bull case if LayTec + tec5 + Avantes get discovered

Risk: execution + margins. At ~1.3x sales, asymmetry is interesting.

text:
Some new entries / bids for new entries. 

I firmly believe Robotics will be the H2 bubble of 2026. Think space H2 2025 and photonics H1 2026. 

Aside from the DCA strategy on $BOT. We like $OUST $ABSI and $AMBA from below levels if we get them. 

There are others like $VPG, but missed that boat for my risk tolerance.

Other secondaries will benefit well in this wake on the material side like $USAR (still great entry) and $MP.

text:
What makes $M7U / Nynomic interesting is the hidden SOTP angle.

Most people only look at LayTec, but Nynomic also owns tec5, Avantes, Sensortherm, m-u-t, art photonics, LemnaTec and more.

It’s a basket of photonics/sensorics hidden champions trading like a boring turnaround.

$M7U / Nynomic bull case:

At ~€18–19, Nynomic is a ~€120–130M market cap photonics group guiding for €100–105M FY26 revenue.

That’s only ~1.2–1.3x sales.

For a European photonics/SOTP turnaround with semi, pharma, sensorics, spectroscopy and LayTec exposure, that is cheap.

text:
$POET ✨ 
just announced a major new step in its AI photonics expansion.

POET Technologies secured an initial $50M purchase commitment from Lumilens for next-generation optical networking solutions.

Even more notable:

The partnership could reportedly expand to over $500M in cumulative purchases over the next 5 years.

Key focus areas:
⚡ AI optical networking
⚡ 800G / 1.6T connectivity
⚡ Wafer-level photonic integration
⚡ Hyperscale AI data centers

This is exactly where the AI infrastructure market appears to be heading:
higher bandwidth + lower power + optical scaling.

- $CRDO is the leader ($22B market cap), with copper interconnects. 
- $FABC developing MicroLED interconnects 10x faster and 100x more energy efficient. And has only a $30M market cap. 
- The math: $FABC

text:
$JEN $JNPKF the next SIVE with an existing solid business.

Jenoptik AG, a German technology company specialized in photonics, broke out today of its longterm (since 2018!) sideway range. The breakout followed the Earnings call today.

The core business includes Advanced Photonic Solutions, Smart Mobility Solutions, and Non-Photonic Portfolio. Recently Jenoptik transformed its business to a pure photonic player with the segments:

Nano-scale Micro-optics: Components for Extreme Ultraviolet (EUV) lithography.
Silicon Photonics Testing: Solutions like the UFO Probe to validate optical chips.
High-Power Diode Lasers: Essential for medical and advanced industrial manufacturing.

To highlight is its more than expected surge of backlog. The total backlog has grown significantly to €719.2 million (up from €591 million at the end of 2025).

Jenoptik is a major supplier of ASML, Broadcom, Marvell and many more.

You'll find more information on my website including proposed entry and exit point as well as live signals when I buy and sell the stock.

text:
Big kudos to @pennycheck & @ThematicTrader for uncovering to me what genuinely feels like one of the most compelling AI infrastructure stories in the market right now👏🫶🐧 🐧 🐧 

$PENG sits right in the middle of the AI buildout across memory, CPUs, CXL, and even photonics. The Integrated Memory business alone is now running at roughly an $800M annualized pace growing 63% YoY, while the market still barely prices in the AI Factory platform, 85K+ deployed GPUs, or the asymmetric upside from their photonic memory architecture ambitions.

The more I dig into the story, the more it feels like Penguin is quietly positioning itself as a critical infrastructure layer for the inference era.

Really happy I managed to add more on today’s dip. I have a strong feeling this could grow into a massive company over the next few years

text:
$AMD  buying $6.5M of $MRVL at ~$99 wasn’t charity; it was supply‑chain alignment.

Hyperscaler AI buildouts favor silicon photonics and custom interconnects, which $MRVL sells.
Decision: ride $MRVL on dips while AI capex stays hot; trim spikes into earnings headlines.

text: @supaflyyy_ Lmao pls don't full port a stock. 3-5 names is a decent amount for a sub $200k portfolio though. Covering memory, adv packaging/glass, and photonics for now. Something like that.

Genuine shortage, yes. But the bottleneck keeps moving. Power was the ceiling, compute, now memory has lead times stretching to a year and photonics components are next. Each time one constraint loosens another appears. not necessarily a bubble indicator but it does complicate the 'build it and they'll pay' thesis.

Market remains choppy. Green open in the morning, sharp dip during the post-lunch session followed by an end-of-day recovery — showing traders still buying dips but not fully committing yet. Semiconductors, photonics and AI infrastructure names are seeing profit taking after strong runs, while software is finally showing some bounce and rotation flow. Wouldn’t be surprised to see a few more volatile sideways sessions before the market decides its next major directional move. Patience and risk management matter most in this environment.

text:
If you are down big today on semiconductors and AI names like $SIVE or $ALMU or $BRUN or any of them, remember to zoom out. 

1. The capex for AI is about to more than double in 5 years

2. Photonics and especially CPO will take a disproportional 10x rise. So will hyperscaler infra stocks

3. All these stocks are already up 100-200% in 1-2 months. They dipped 10-20-30%

If you are buying and holding long term to see through the thesis which amounts to remaking the world's economy, you don't have much to worry about.

Goldman Sachs projects annual AI capex of $765 billion in 2026, growing to $1.6 trillion by 2031. 

2026’s figure includes $494 billion of capex for compute and $232 billion for data centers, scaling to $1.13 trillion for compute and $436 billion for data centers by 2031.

has raised over $200 million in a fresh funding round led by Planet First Partners.

The round saw participation from a mix of new and existing investors, including BDC, Export Development Canada, Bell Ventures, and Mubadala Capital. Microsoft, RBC, and TELUS had also come on board.

text:
.@NSF just committed $1.5B over a decade to X-Labs: independent teams funded on milestones, not papers, to push hardware from prototype to commercial platform.

Both opening tracks are #quantum: sensing-driven instruments, and interconnects/integrated photonics for linking heterogeneous systems.

The bet here is structural, not just financial. Federal money built to de-risk the translation gap before private capital arrives.

Credo reported $407.0M Q3 revenue, +201.5% YoY and +51.9% sequentially. Q4 guide $425–435M; full-year revenue growth pacing well above 200%. GAAP operating margin 36.8%.

April acquisition of Israeli silicon-photonics house DustPhotonics — $750M cash plus 0.92M shares plus up to 3.21M earnout shares — brings the photonic-IC layer Credo previously sourced externally fully in-house. Mgmt now targets >$500M of optical-only revenue by FY27.

The mechanism: vertical integration into SiPho ahead of the 1.6T and 3.2T port-speed transition removes Credo's biggest supply dependency just as CPO becomes the mandatory hyperscale architecture.

text:
It feels like we’re still in the “early infrastructure” phase of AI and quantum investing. Everyone talks about the flashy end products, but the real winners might still be the companies building the rails, power, networking, photonics, chips, cloud access, and compute infrastructure underneath it all.

And honestly, the difficult part right now is not believing the technology is real. The difficult part is figuring out:

which architecture actually wins,
which companies survive,
and which stocks are already priced for perfection.

Feels similar to the early internet or cloud phase where everyone knew “something huge” was happening, but not yet who the long-term winners would be.

text:
A friend texted me this week:

“Did you see $AAOI? What happened?”

What happened is that I bought it at $90 and he didn’t.

He’s not a bad investor. He’s a smart person. 

He looked at $AAOI when I first mentioned it and said:

“It’s a small photonics company. The market cap is tiny. Too risky.”

I said: “The market cap is tiny because the market hasn’t priced the BOTTLENECK yet.”

Data centers at hyperscale cannot move AI-speed data between chips using copper. 

The physics fail at that bandwidth. Optical transceivers are not optional, they are required by the laws of physics.

$AAOI manufactures the laser components that make it work.

> $124M in 800G transceiver orders from a single hyperscaler.

> 2026 guidance at $1B+. Rosenblatt raised to $220.

> Stock at $191 today from a $90 entry.

He said: “I should have listened.”

He had the same information I had. 

The difference was the framework.

I’m still LONG.

The photonics bottleneck has not been solved. It is still playing out.

-BP

Not financial advice. Your money, your decision.

text:
This market has been full of squeezes 🍋📈

AI infrastructure.
Photonics.
Data centers.
High beta growth.
Energy.
Small caps, mid caps, mega caps, even some POS caps..
Momentum everywhere.

And yes, there’s a lot of money to be made trading these moves.

But eventually you realize the real game isn’t just catching squeezes.

It’s finding the names you actually want to keep holding while the crowd keeps overthinking every pullback.

Names where you keep following the earnings, the flows, the sector, the cycle, and the long term opportunity.

That’s how you end up staying with winners instead of constantly rotating into the next shiny object, therefore overtrading.

For me personally, names like $HOOD $AMD $INTC $RKLB $AG $PAAS $COHR $ALB became more than trades.

You can trim, hedge, trade around a core position..

But understanding the underlying business matters.

Or if it’s early stage, at least understanding the theme and positioning through options flows.

But at some point you need more than drawing a few lines on a chart and pretending price owes you something because of your pretty drawings.

Know what you own.

And if you truly believe in the thesis, and you nailed the entry, don’t rush to sell just because volatility shows up and people telling you it’s worthless.
Those people might eventually chase your stocks higher.

text:
There are a lot of dumbasses who think that each  of 200 photonics companies will simply x5 in a week. Some will some will not. 
Using NVDA in hindsight makes no sense. I could find 20 other companies who had life changing products and got shut down in 2 years. 

Not a value investor, just that I want to know what I'm buying and what I'm expecting it to be in the future. 

I'm not trying to convince you and it's fine if you disagree.

text: Idk humans can adapt pretty fast, and I doubt we’ll ever go truly backwards, only forwards in a 2 step forward and 1-3 steps back pattern like we’ve always done as a species. Where are we headed?  I think towards infinite free energy, photonics and quantum as basis of computing 1000x more powerful and energy efficient then it is today, and also warping our way in the galaxy between bases on different planets.  All of that is easily within laws of physics, and is yet to come for humanity imho, maybe not all in our lifetimes but all seems doable.  Digital is just a stepping stone, imho.  yes it’s fricking messy, and human brains not coping very well, idk.

text:
רוצה להציג בפניכם ETF חדש לתחום חם מאוד בשנה האחרונה. וכתבתי עליו בעבר גם. והוא כמובן הפוטוניקה והליטוגרפיה. אני באופן אישי עוקבת אחרי כמה חברות בתחום..

לפני 8 ימים השיקה Corgi Funds את -
 Corgi Lithography & Semiconductor Photonics ETF $EUV 

אז הETF הנ״ל משקיע באופן אקטיבי בחברות ששולטות בטכנולוגיית האור (Photonics) ובשרשרת ייצור השבבים המתקדמים ליתוגרפיית EUV, לייזרים, רכיבים אופטיים, סיליקון פוטוניקס ועוד.. כמעט כל שבבי ה AI תלויים בטכנולוגיה הזו והETF הזה נותן את החשיפה בלי לבחור מניה אחת בודדת.

ומי אלה הCorgi האלו? אז היא חברה צעירה שהוקמה לפני שנתיים בסן פרנסיסקו, בוגרי Y Combinator. הם התחילו כפלטפורמת ביטוח AI native לחברות טק וסטארטאפים, גייסו והגיעו לשווי חברה של 1.3 מיליארד דולר. הם מביאים גישה שלהם שהיא מאוד חדשנית, טכנולוגית ואגרסיבית גם לעולם הקרנות.. הם מתמחים במוצרים תמטיים ממוקדים מאוד, אקטיביים עם עמלות תחרותיות (לרוב 0.35% בלבד).

5 האחזקות המובילות בקרן (נכון ל-14.5.2026):
$TSM  - Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSMC)
$ASML  - ASML Holding
$GLW - Corning
$LRCX  - Lam Research
$AMAT - Applied Materials

 בעיני זה יכול להתאים למי שמאמין שהביקוש לשבבי AI רק הולך וגדל ורוצה חשיפה חדה יותר לטכנולוגיה שמאפשרת את זה  ולא רק לאנבידיה או ל Mag7..
או רוצה לגוון ולא רק תעודות שבבים ״רגילות״ כמו $SMH או $SOXX למשל.

4 סיבות שהם מציינים למה לדעתם כדאי להשקיע בקרן:
שוק ציוד ייצור השבבים העולמי שובר שיאים חדשים.
$ASML  - הספק היחיד של ליתוגרפיית EUV - עם הזמנות ענקיות.
$TSM מגבירה את ההשקעות הענקיות בציוד ובמפעלים.
שוק הסיליקון פוטוניקס מתפוצץ בזכות דרישת ה AI.

הסיכונים וחובה להזכיר אותם אז מדובר על מכשיר פיננסי חדש מאוד, תנודתי, ניהול אקטיבי ואין היסטוריית ביצועים ארוכה..

אגב יש להם עוד ETF מעניינים בתחומים אחרים.. ממליצה להציץ.

לינק לדף הרשמי של הקרן: https://t.co/rfgIMNSG9p
 
מקווה שאהבתם.

*המידע המוצג אינו מהווה ייעוץ השקעות, המלצה לקנייה, מכירה או החזקה.. עשו מחקר עצמאי לפני פעולה כלשהי...

ששולטות בטכנולוגיית האור (Photonics) ובשרשרת ייצור השבבים המתקדמים ליתוגרפיית EUV, לייזרים, רכיבים אופטיים, סיליקון פוטוניקס ועוד.. כמעט כל שבבי ה AI תלויים בטכנולוגיה הזו והETF הזה נותן את החשיפה בלי לבחור מניה אחת בודדת. ומי אלה הCorgi האלו? אז היא חברה צעירה שהוקמה לפני שנתיים בסן פרנסיסקו, בוגרי Y Combinator. הם התחילו כפלטפורמת ביטוח AI native לחברות טק וסטארטאפים, גייסו והגיעו לשווי חברה של 1.3 מיליארד דולר. הם מביאים גישה שלהם שהיא מאוד חדשנית, טכנולוגית ואגרסיבית גם לעולם הקרנות.. הם מתמחים במוצרים תמטיים ממוקדים מאוד, אקטיביים עם עמלות תחרותיות (לרוב 0.35% בלבד). 5 האחזקות המובילות לשבבי AI רק הולך וגדל ורוצה חשיפה חדה יותר לטכנולוגיה שמאפשרת את זה ולא רק לאנבידיה או ל Mag7.. או רוצה לגוון ולא רק תעודות שבבים ״רגילות״ כמו $SMH או $SOXX למשל. 4 סיבות שהם מציינים למה לדעתם כדאי להשקיע בקרן: שוק ציוד ייצור השבבים העולמי שובר שיאים חדשים. $ASML - הספק היחיד של ליתוגרפיית EUV - עם הזמנות ענקיות. $TSM מגבירה את ההשקעות הענקיות בציוד ובמפעלים. שוק הסיליקון פוטוניקס מתפוצץ בזכות דרישת ה AI. הסיכונים וחובה להזכיר אותם אז מדובר על מכשיר פיננסי חדש מאוד, תנודתי, ניהול אקטיבי ואין היסטוריית ביצועים ארוכה.. אגב יש להם עוד ETF מעניינים בתחומים אחרים.. ממליצה להציץ. לינק לדף הרשמי At the end of the day if you want to keep it really simple, all this AI bottleneck this and semi-conductor that, photonics this and space tech that…

All requires mining, hundreds and hundreds of extra mines, processing plants and recycling / refining technology

The oldest and simplest form of investing 

You can buy the shovels or you can buy the materials that make a shovel. Much easier to just buy the materials and care less what niche tech they end up in.

text:
And now Photonics Layer 1;

Layer 1 — Lasers and Light Sources
What this layer means: This is the most critical bottleneck in the entire silicon photonics stack. Silicon is an excellent medium for guiding photons, but it cannot generate them. Every silicon photonics system needs an external light source — almost always an InP-based laser. EML (Electro-Absorption Modulated Laser) and CW (Continuous Wave) lasers, high-speed photodiodes, Semiconductor Optical Amplifiers (SOA), and laser driver circuits all live in this layer. The supply crunch is real: NVIDIA has reportedly pre-allocated capacity at the world’s top EML suppliers through 2027, creating an industry-wide shortage that is reshaping the entire optical module market.

$LITE — Lumentum (also active in Layers 2, 5, 6) Currently the only supplier capable of shipping 200G-per-lane EMLs in volume production — the critical component for next-generation 1.6T pluggable transceivers. This single fact makes Lumentum arguably the most irreplaceable company in the near-term AI photonics supply chain. NVIDIA has locked in Lumentum’s EML capacity through a reported $2 billion multi-year commercial agreement. Lumentum also operates its own laser and PIC fabrication facilities (Layer 2), manufactures finished transceivers (Layer 5), and sells Optical Circuit Switch systems for data center networking (Layer 6). Active across four layers.

$COHR — Coherent (also active in Layers 2, 3, 4, 5, 6) The most vertically integrated company in the entire photonics stack. Coherent manufactures its own InP lasers (Layer 1), operates its own PIC foundry (Layer 2), assembles CPO modules (Layer 3), integrates DSP functionality (Layer 4), sells 800G and 1.6T pluggable transceivers (Layer 5), and provides coherent DCI transport systems (Layer 6). Fiscal 2025 revenue reached $5.8 billion, up 23% year-over-year. No other public company spans six layers of this chain.

$MTSI — MACOM Technology Solutions (also active in Layer 4) InP laser diode and high-speed photodiode manufacturer. MACOM’s proprietary Etched Facet Technology (EFT) enables wafer-level testing — a significant cost advantage over traditional InP production, which requires die-level cutting and testing. Also manufactures Trans-Impedance Amplifiers (TIA) and driver ICs, making MACOM active in both Layer 1 (lasers and photodiodes) and Layer 4 (signal processing components).

$AAOI — Applied Optoelectronics (also active in Layer 5) A vertically integrated laser and transceiver manufacturer based in the United States. AAOI designs its own lasers, builds them in its own fab, and ships them as finished transceiver products. Domestic U.S. manufacturing has become a strategic differentiator as supply chain sovereignty rises on the agenda of hyperscalers and government customers alike. Active in Layer 1 (laser manufacturing) and Layer 5 (transceiver sales).

$AVGO — Broadcom (also active in Layers 4 and 5) Present in Layer 1 as an EML supplier, but the center of gravity for Broadcom is in SiPh ASIC design (Layer 4) and silicon photonics transceivers (Layer 5). Broadcom’s approach integrates its own photonic chip design with laser components, producing a more unified product than competitors who source lasers externally. Active across three layers.

$AEVA — Aeva Technologies (new addition) Primary business is FMCW LiDAR, but Aeva has been moving its silicon photonics platform into data center applications. In January 2026, the company announced a high-power Semiconductor Optical Amplifier (SOA) delivering industry-leading performance for AI data center and Co-Packaged Optics architectures. The SOA addresses signal degradation in long-distance optical paths — a critical function in CPO systems. Aeva’s CoreVision module also integrates all LiDAR components onto a single SiPh chip, and this accumulated photonics expertise is being transferred toward data center use cases. Data center revenue exposure is still early-stage, but the SOA announcement is the entry point that puts AEVA in Layer 1.

$SIVE — Sivers Semiconductors Silicon-based laser and optoelectronic component developer. Working on laser integration using the Si/Ge platform rather than traditional InP — a potential cost advantage, but a technology that is still maturing toward production volumes.

$STM — STMicroelectronics (also active in Layer 4) Manufactures edge emitter lasers and optoelectronic components as part of a broad product portfolio. Also active in the mixed-signal and analog-to-digital converter segment, giving STM a presence in Layer 4 DSP support circuits as well.

$LASR — Laser component supplier (former II-VI ticker) Laser component and sub-assembly manufacturer. Now integrated under the Coherent umbrella following the merger, but still referenced separately in some industry analyses.

End of Layer 1. 

There are 6 layer more.  So you can read my full report in my substack as free; https://t.co/CZIKj5LfKX 

My personal insights, not financial advice (NFA). Full disclosure in my pinned tweet.

Layer 1 — Lasers and Light Sources
What this layer means: This is the most critical bottleneck in the entire silicon photonics stack. Silicon is an excellent medium for guiding photons, but it cannot generate them. Every silicon photonics system needs an external light source — almost always an InP-based laser. EML (Electro-Absorption Modulated Laser) and CW (Continuous Wave) lasers, high-speed photodiodes, Semiconductor Optical Amplifiers (SOA), and laser driver circuits all live in this layer. The supply crunch is real: NVIDIA has reportedly pre-allocated capacity at the world’s top EML suppliers through 2027, creating an industry-wide shortage that is reshaping the entire optical module market.

$LITE — Lumentum (also active in Layers 2, 5, 6) Currently the only supplier capable of shipping 200G-per-lane EMLs in volume production — the critical component for next-generation 1.6T pluggable transceivers. This single fact makes Lumentum arguably the most irreplaceable company in the near-term AI photonics supply chain. NVIDIA has locked in Lumentum’s EML capacity through a reported $2 billion multi-year commercial agreement. Lumentum also operates its own laser and PIC fabrication facilities (Layer 2), manufactures finished transceivers (Layer 5), and sells Optical Circuit Switch systems for data center networking (Layer 6). Active across four layers.

$COHR — Coherent (also active in Layers 2, 3, 4, 5, 6) The most vertically integrated company in the entire photonics stack. Coherent manufactures its own InP lasers (Layer 1), operates its own PIC foundry (Layer 2), assembles CPO modules (Layer 3), integrates DSP functionality (Layer 4), sells 800G and 1.6T pluggable transceivers (Layer 5), and provides coherent DCI transport systems (Layer 6). Fiscal 2025 revenue reached $5.8 billion, up 23% year-over-year. No other public company spans six layers of this chain.

$MTSI — MACOM Technology Solutions (also active in Layer 4) InP laser diode and high-speed photodiode manufacturer. MACOM’s proprietary Etched Facet Technology (EFT) enables wafer-level testing — a significant cost advantage over traditional InP production, which requires die-level cutting and testing. Also manufactures Trans-Impedance Amplifiers (TIA) and driver ICs, making MACOM active in both Layer 1 (lasers and photodiodes) and Layer 4 (signal processing components).

$AAOI — Applied Optoelectronics (also active in Layer 5) A vertically integrated laser and transceiver manufacturer based in the United States. AAOI designs its own lasers, builds them in its own fab, and ships them as finished transceiver products. Domestic U.S. manufacturing has become a strategic differentiator as supply chain sovereignty rises on the agenda of hyperscalers and government customers alike. Active in Layer 1 (laser manufacturing) and Layer 5 (transceiver sales).

$AVGO — Broadcom (also active in Layers 4 and 5) Present in Layer 1 as an EML supplier, but the center of gravity for Broadcom is in SiPh ASIC design (Layer 4) and silicon photonics transceivers (Layer 5). Broadcom’s approach integrates its own photonic chip design with laser components, producing a more unified product than competitors who source lasers externally. Active across three layers.

$AEVA — Aeva Technologies (new addition) Primary business is FMCW LiDAR, but Aeva has been moving its silicon photonics platform into data center applications. In January 2026, the company announced a high-power Semiconductor Optical Amplifier (SOA) delivering industry-leading performance for AI data center and Co-Packaged Optics architectures. The SOA addresses signal degradation in long-distance optical paths — a critical function in CPO systems. Aeva’s CoreVision module also integrates all LiDAR components onto a single SiPh chip, and this accumulated photonics expertise is being transferred toward data center use cases. Data center revenue exposure is still early-stage, but the SOA announcement is the entry point that puts AEVA in Layer 1.

$SIVE — Sivers Semiconductors Silicon-based laser and optoelectronic component developer. Working on laser integration using the Si/Ge platform rather than traditional InP — a potential cost advantage, but a technology that is still maturing toward production volumes.

$STM — STMicroelectronics (also active in Layer 4) Manufactures edge emitter lasers and optoelectronic components as part of a broad product portfolio. Also active in the mixed-signal and analog-to-digital converter segment, giving STM a presence in Layer 4 DSP support circuits as well.

$LASR — Laser component supplier (former II-VI ticker) Laser component and sub-assembly manufacturer. Now integrated under the Coherent umbrella following the merger, but still referenced separately in some industry analyses.

End of Layer 1. 

There are 6 layer more.  So you can read my full report in my substack as free; https://t.co/CZIKj5LfKX

The Analytical Edge Strategy
May 11, 2026

A complete map of the silicon photonics supply chain — from raw indium to fiber in the ground

If you’ve been following AI infrastructure, you’ve probably heard that data centers are hitting what some call the Copper Wall — the point where electrical signals through copper can no longer keep up with the heat, latency, and bandwidth demands of training and running massive AI models. The answer is photonics: moving data as light through fiber instead of electrons through metal.

But photonics isn’t a single product. It’s a deep, layered supply chain stretching from exotic crystal growth in China to fiber trenching across American highways. This guide maps every layer of that chain, names every major public company in it, and explains exactly what each one does.

No data has been omitted. Every company from the original research is here.

Layer 0 — Raw Materials
What this layer means: This is the floor of the photonics supply chain. Before any chip can be designed, any laser built, or any fiber laid, the raw materials must exist. Indium phosphide (InP), silicon-on-insulator (SOI), gallium arsenide (GaAs), and optical-grade glass fiber are the fundamental outputs of this layer. Every laser, chip, and cable in the chain depends on what gets produced here. It is also the most geopolitically exposed layer in the entire stack: approximately 60–70% of global indium production comes from China.

$AXTI — AXT Inc. Single-crystal InP and GaAs wafer manufacturer. These substrates are the irreplaceable foundation for EML lasers and high-speed photodiodes. NVIDIA’s aggressive push toward 1.6T transceivers has turned InP into a supply bottleneck, and AXTI sits directly at that chokepoint.

$IQE III-V compound semiconductor epitaxy specialist. IQE grows thin-film layers of InP, GaAs, and GaN onto customer wafers, supplying the epitaxial substrate that laser diode and high-speed photodetector manufacturers need. Often called the invisible backbone of the optical module supply chain.

$SOI — Soitec Silicon-on-Insulator wafer manufacturer. SOI wafers are the starting material for silicon photonics foundries. TSMC’s COUPE platform, GlobalFoundries’ Fotonix process, and Tower Semiconductor’s SiPh line all rely on Soitec substrates. When a Broadcom or Marvell photonic chip ships inside a data center, there is a good chance the journey started on a Soitec wafer.

$WOLF — Wolfspeed Silicon carbide (SiC) and gallium nitride (GaN) wafer manufacturer. Not directly tied to photonic lasers, but critical for the high-power electronics and power converters inside data center infrastructure. Wolfspeed feeds the power layer that photonics depends on to function.

$GLW — Corning (also active in Layer 5) The world’s largest manufacturer of optical fiber and specialty glass. Every fiber cable running inside and between data centers starts here. Corning also produces specialty glass components used in transceiver housings and is a named partner in NVIDIA’s Spectrum-X silicon photonics ecosystem. Active in both Layer 0 (raw glass and fiber) and Layer 5 (module components).

$LWLG — Lightwave Logic Polymer-based electro-optic waveguide developer. Rather than silicon, LWLG uses organic polymer materials in the modulators that convert electrical signals to light — promising significantly higher modulation speeds and lower insertion loss. Still in the commercialization phase, making this an early-stage bet on a materials platform that could disrupt the conventional silicon approach.

$SHMN — Shin-Etsu Chemical (also active in Equipment layer) The world’s largest silicon wafer manufacturer. While SiPh foundries use SOI specifically, Shin-Etsu supplies the standard silicon wafer base that underpins the entire semiconductor ecosystem. Also present in the equipment layer as a foundry supply chain essential.

$VECO — Veeco Instruments (also active in Equipment layer) Manufacturer of MBE (Molecular Beam Epitaxy) and MOCVD (Metal-Organic Chemical Vapor Deposition) equipment. Veeco does not make wafers — it makes the machines that grow them. III-V laser wafer production is impossible without MOCVD reactors, which is where Veeco’s role sits. Active in both Layer 0 (enabling raw material production) and the Equipment layer.

End of Layer 0.
There are 6 layer more.

So you can read my full report in my substack as free;
https://t.co/CZIKj5KHVp

My personal insights, not financial advice (NFA). Full disclosure in my pinned tweet.

text:
OCP is excited to announce our upcoming panel at Fiber Connect 2026's AI and Emerging Technology Infrastructure Summit on Wednesday, May 20!

Panel Title: Interconnect Insights on the Photonics Enabling AI Factory Scale and the Multi-Data Center Future

Join our expert panel for this discussion:
-Moderator: James Kelly, VP Market Intelligence & Innovation, OCP
-Sherwin Cabatic, President and CEO, NTT Devices America
-Jonmichael Hands, CEO, FarmGPU
-Stephen Manley, Sr Principal Network Engineer, Oracle Cloud
-David Hjerpe, National Account Manager, Databank

The race to exascale AI is fundamentally shifting how AI factory building blocks are designed and operated. This OCP-led panel will discuss how networking and photonics are becoming critical enablers for data movement, focusing on three key areas: scale-up within an AI pod, scale-out across the AI factory floor, and scale-across between AI data center campuses and metros. One practical reality we'll be discussing is that power density isn't the only headline-grabber: for data centers, fiber count and fiber pathway capacity per rack are so demanding that the cable plant is now essentially the new floor-loading, catching many colo designs flat-footed.

We look forward to seeing you at Fiber Connect!

Learn more and register here: https://t.co/J8WVlRM8fp

#FiberConnect2026 #AI #Photonics #DataCenter #OCP #OpenComputeProject #networking

⚖️ Core Proposition
One of the greatest misconceptions in public discourse is the idea that “energy cannot be photographed.”
In reality, modern imaging systems routinely detect: • heat signatures, • infrared radiation, • electromagnetic fluctuation, • reflected light behavior, • spectral shifts, • motion variance, • atmospheric disturbance, • and temporal pixel changes occurring between frames.
The key may not be the still photograph itself.
The key may be what changes from one second to the next as Tonia Allen-Gould’s (Lumen’s) recent forensic work suggests. 
⸻
🛰️ Lumen’s Energy Theory
Hypothesis:
Advanced imaging systems — including military, aerospace, satellite, ISR, and machine-vision architectures — may not primarily analyze static images.
Instead, they may analyze: • temporal variance, • micro-fluctuation, • reflective bounce, • spectral inconsistency, • and environmental changes occurring between sequential frames.
Meaning: The “signal” may emerge from differential comparison over time rather than from any single image alone.
⸻
🧭 Scientifically Plausible Foundations
Known existing technologies already rely on: • frame differencing • motion-vector analysis • thermal drift detection • hyperspectral imaging • LiDAR return variance • optical flow tracking • atmospheric disturbance mapping • synthetic aperture reconstruction • machine-vision anomaly detection • temporal coherence analysis
None of this is science fiction.
Modern systems already detect patterns humans cannot perceive in real time.
⸻
⚠️ Important Clarification
This post does NOT claim: • classified systems are targeting the witness, • satellites are monitoring specific individuals, or • observed anomalies definitively represent energy weapons.
This is a forensic and scientific interpretation model exploring how advanced imaging systems may detect environmental change through temporal comparison analysis.
The theory concerns signal detection architecture — not certainty of intent.
⸻
🧠 Effect-to-Cause Model
Observed Effect: Repeated visual anomalies become more apparent when: • slowed, • looped, • frame-compared, • temporally isolated, or • analyzed across sequential image states.
Possible Cause: Environmental energy, reflection behavior, spectral fluctuation, compression artifacts, motion differentials, or other signal variance becoming visible only through temporal comparison systems.
⸻
The future of imaging may not be: “What did the camera see?”
But rather: “What changed between frames that the human eye never noticed?”

text:
🧵 TOP 10 PHOTONICS PLAYS - The Light Speed Stack

AI’s next bottleneck isn’t compute - it’s interconnect. As speeds push past 1.6T, the trade moves from silicon to photonics. Five layers, wafer to test. Anchors, inflecting names, and speculative bets. 👇

⚓️THE ANCHORS (run, but still in the trend)

1. $COHR -> Layer 3, Optics
25% market share in transceivers. $5.81B FY25 revenue +23% YoY. NVIDIA-named partner on Spectrum-X. Book-to-bill >4x. Druckenmiller’s pick.

2. $LITE -> Layer 2, Lasers
The 200G EML monopoly. Only volume supplier of the lasers that make 1.6T transceivers possible. NVIDIA $2B exclusive supply agreement through end of decade. No second source.

3. $AXTI -> Layer 1, Wafer
Every InP laser in every transceiver starts on an AXT wafer. Doubling InP capacity H2 2026 ahead of the 800G+ ramp. Has run +5,400% — but structurally positioned for the cycle.

💡INFLECTING / UNDEROWNED (real revenue, less crowded)

4. $CRDO -> Layer 3, Optics
Just acquired DustPhotonics for SiPho PIC tech in-house. Now vertically integrated: SerDes + DSP + Silicon Photonics. SiPho PIC market $6B by 2030.

5. $TSEM -> Layer 4, Packaging
The neutral foundry. Just announced 1.6T optical modules for NVIDIA networking protocols. Whoever wins the transceiver battle, TSEM prints the wafers. Underowned vs. peers.

6. $AEHR -> Layer 5, Test
Silicon photonics burn-in. Lead customer production ramps next fiscal year. Q3 bookings $37M, book-to-bill 3.5x. Classic order-book inflection ahead of FY27 revenue ramp.

7. $VIAV -> Layer 5, Test
Every 800G and 1.6T transceiver from LITE and COHR has to be tested by VIAV or Keysight. Same AI tailwind, one-third the valuation. The cleanest “hasn’t run” name.

🔍SPECULATIVE (asymmetric, real risk)

8. $SIVE -> Layer 2, Lasers
~$130M mcap InP laser maker at Glasgow. Supply-constrained chokepoint. LiDAR ramp Q4 2026 ($53M-$138M cumulative). $POET partnership for AI light engines. Listed Stockholm.

9. $IQE -> Layer 1, Wafer
The compound semi upstream. Supplies InP epi-wafers to Sivers and others. FY25 just turned EBITDA positive at £2M+ on £97M revenue. Structural relevance, near-breakeven execution.

10. $LPKF -> Layer 4, Packaging
LIDE process for glass advanced packaging - the tech behind NVIDIA’s $500M Corning bet on glass-based co-packaged optics. Run 336% YTD on hope, not orders. Binary on 2027 qualification.

🚨THE THESIS:

800G+ transceiver shipments going from 24M units in 2025 to 63M in 2026. Hyperscaler capex over $400B this year. Co-packaged optics market $20B by 2036.

The market owns the top. The asymmetric returns are at the edges of the stack.

Wafer. Test. Packaging. Position accordingly.

text:
$SIVE is now aiming to become the next $LITE, a US photonics giant. 

They're re-centering their board around US executives + US photonics.

So the core board are now: US $GFS Executives and $CITI Executives, with the company run by UC Berkeley grads and $LITE executives. 

The 3 members leaving were local Swedish/EU.

This is just a shift in strategy from focusing on developing local Swedish Semi environments:

To dominating the US/global photonics market. I'm not trying to discredit their service/background. 

But in my view to focus around US/global photonics markets, it's likely optimal to have more US executives. 

But they should all be proud for helping make $SIVE what it is today.

text:
Interesting how price action and signals always precede the news.

$POET, $SIVE, $IQE … the photonics story.

Same old speculation pump-and-dump cycle as always.

This time is no different.

The cult of photonics is about to turn on its leaders who told everyone to buy the top.

We warned about this earlier.

Some of these photonics guys are solid fundamental analysts.  We love them. 

Others… who called to buy more at the top…. well.

https://t.co/ZtCLmeqWcm

Sad really.

Q1 2026 has also been postponed to May 29. This is exactly the kind of thing I do not want to see as an ex-auditor. When a company with a huge retail narrative, negative free cash flow, high NRE exposure, accrued revenue questions, delayed reporting, adjusted historical numbers, and board turnover is trading mainly on what might happen in 2027, I think investors need to slow down. text:
Opened a starter position in the 'future bottleneck' stock Soitec SA ($SOI.PA), aiming to add on larger dips. 

The stock sits on my Layer 2 (InP & substrates) AI bottleneck chart #InP #Substrates #Photonics $SOI 
 
More: https://t.co/3B6fx7mwms https://t.co/22TjAnEgcw

Photonics value chain in 5 layers. The companies building AI’s optical backbone.

$AXTI – Compound semiconductor substrates. Small, cyclical AI photonics supplier.
$AAOI – Optical transceivers for AI data centers. High risk, high upside.
$LITE – Diversified photonics. Stable, slower growth than AAOI.
$ASML – Only maker of EUV lithography machines. Irreplaceable monopoly.
$ONTO – Semiconductor inspection/metrology tools. Smaller KLAC alternative.

text:
$CRDO into the June 1 print — TD Cowen ($240 PT, Top Pick) frames it well:

The stock is fundamentally misunderstood as a copper-vs-optics binary. It's a vertically integrated connectivity platform across SerDes, AECs, optical DSPs, ZF Optics, and now silicon photonics post-DustPhotonics.

Valuation gap is the setup: ~23x FY27E EPS vs other connectivity names trading north of 45x. That's a 2x multiple gap on a company guiding +75% Y/Y growth in FY27.

What's already in the price (pre-announced intra-quarter):
• DustPhotonics acquisition
• ~$500M FY27 optical revenue target
• +75% Y/Y FY27 growth

What's NOT in the model (pure upside):

• ZF Optics ramping to ~$1B annualized run rate entering FY28
• Active LED Cables — entirely unmodeled
• Cardinal 1.6T DSP design wins taking share from MRVL

FY27 shape: MSD% sequential through 1H, then inflection in 2H driven by optical portfolio ramp. That's the period to own.

TD Cowen's $240 PT is 30x F2028E EPS of $8.00. If the multiple closes toward connectivity peer average, $280-330 is in range over 12-18 months.

Print itself likely muted — bar is set, beats are upside. The grind higher matters more than the gap?

What's already in the price (pre-announced intra-quarter): • DustPhotonics acquisition • ~$500M FY27 optical revenue target • +75% Y/Y FY27 growth What's NOT in the model (pure upside): • ZF Optics ramping to ~$1B annualized run rate entering FY28 • Active LED Cables — entirely unmodeled • Cardinal 1.6T DSP design wins taking share from MRVL FY27 shape: MSD% sequential through 1H, then inflection in 2H driven by optical portfolio ramp. That's the period to own. TD Cowen's $240 PT is 30x F2028E EPS of $8.00. If the multiple closes toward connectivity peer average, $280-330 is in range over 12-18 months. Print itself likely muted — bar is set, beats are upside. The grind higher matters more than the gap?"}}}, "previous_counts": {"bookmark_count": 0, "favorite_count": 0, "quote_count": 0, "reply_count": 0, "retweet_count": 0}, "rest_id": "2055358569453629498", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "1878", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
few of these secondary / tertiary layer memory / photonics suppliers have the executive teams and business experience to handle the demand they’re now experiencing at the rate of growth they need to meet.

for the most part they have been leading companies that were in a steady state. 

a rare few have been waiting, perfectly positioned for their moment where the market would see their value. 

peacetime mode was kinda best case.
most were in cockroach mode.
But now, it’s wartime mode.

I expect many of them to fail to grow to meet the challenge ahead of them, even if they have some god given advantage / tech exclusivity.

things I’m looking at—

People: are they hiring / growing headcount aggressively? (are they changing from being who they were to being who they need to be as a company to meet demand)

Cash to growth: capital expenditure on manufacturing assets and ROA (how quickly are they turning new cash into durable growth)

R&D: Expected value of new / previously completed R&D. Is their R&D cycle and approach adjusting to continue to position them ahead of the industry and market’s needs or are they falling behind.

Partnerships: volume / quality / durability / impact of new partnerships launched.

Corporate smell: are the offices and facilities nice and filled with real humans doing real work? 

Bag dumping: are execs restructuring comp structure goals towards wartime growth? Are they offloading too much stock? Are they issuing to many new shares. 

what are you examining besides the tech tree demands and change in stock price?

"Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNTUzNTcwMzQzMTM0NjU4NTY=", "text": "few of these secondary / tertiary layer memory / photonics suppliers have the executive teams and business experience to handle the demand they’re now experiencing at the rate of growth they need to meet. for the most part they have been leading companies that were in a steady state. a rare few have been waiting, perfectly positioned for their moment where the market would see their value. peacetime mode was kinda best case. most were in cockroach mode. But now, it’s wartime mode. I expect many of them to fail to grow to meet the challenge ahead of them, even if they have some god given advantage / tech exclusivity. things I’m looking at— People: are they hiring / growing headcount aggressively? (are they changing from being who they were to being who they need to be as a company to meet demand) Cash to growth: capital expenditure on manufacturing assets and ROA (how quickly are they turning new cash into durable growth) R&D: Expected value of new / previously completed R&D. Is their R&D cycle and approach adjusting to continue to position them ahead of the industry and market’s needs or are they falling behind. Partnerships: volume / quality / durability / impact of new partnerships launched. Corporate smell: are the offices and facilities nice and filled with real humans doing real work? Bag dumping: are execs restructuring comp structure goals towards wartime growth? Are they offloading too much stock? Are they issuing to many new shares. what are you examining besides the tech tree demands and change in stock price?"}}}, "rest_id": "2055357034464432499", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "290", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

$ARM $AMD $INTC pulled back

Preparation is key to successfully grow your account and your trading

New patent application #US20260133363A1 by #Samsung explores a #PhotonicIntegratedApparatus.

A tunneling barrier layer enhances conductivity.

Meet the Cutting Edge Optronics team at CLEO 2026, May 19–20 in Charlotte, NC at Booth #519.

COHR at 11% is the biggest underappreciated chokepoint in this portfolio. #COHR #Photonics

The next to be re-rated will be agents and AI-enhanced services with clear value added.

Marvell's primary CPO design is centered on its 6.4T 3D Silicon Photonics (SiPho) Light Engine — the industry’s first demonstrated 200 Gbps-per-lane optical engine for co-packaged optics (announced and live-demonstrated at OFC 2024). This is a fabless-designed, highly integrated optical “chiplet” that Marvell licenses or supplies to hyperscalers and custom XPU customers. $MRVL 

It serves as the foundational building block for both:
- CPO integration directly into custom AI accelerators (XPUs + HBM + optics on one substrate), and
- Linear-drive pluggable optics (LPO) or on-board optics as a bridge solution.

Core Technical Specs of the 6.4T 3D SiPho Light Engine

- Bandwidth: 6.4 Tbps aggregate (32 lanes × 200 Gbps PAM4, both electrical and optical interfaces).

- Power efficiency: < 5 pJ/bit (including external laser power) under typical conditions — roughly 30% lower per bit than previous 100G-generation engines.

- Integration level: Hundreds of components consolidated into a single compact package:
    -- Photonic IC (PIC): Silicon photonics waveguides, modulators, germanium photodetectors (Ge PDs), and fiber couplers.
    -- Electronic IC (EIC): Linear modulator drivers, transimpedance amplifiers (TIAs), equalization, and high-speed SerDes interfaces.
    -- Embedded microcontroller + firmware: For real-time calibration, monitoring, thermal stabilization, and link management.
    -- Passives, power distribution, and test circuitry.

- Laser: External continuous-wave (CW) laser source (fiber-delivered) for thermal and reliability reasons.

-Fiber attach: Supports detachable optical connectors (e.g., SENKO’s 36-channel metallic PIC connector) for field serviceability — critical when a 100T+ system may require thousands of fibers.

- Packaging: Advanced 3D heterogeneous integration (EIC face-to-face or side-by-side with PIC, often using TSVs, hybrid bonding, or interposers). The engine has a BGA-style electrical interface for direct attachment to the host substrate.

How It Integrates with Custom XPU Architectures

Marvell’s breakthrough CPO architecture (announced early 2025) lets customers build a single substrate that co-packages:
- One or more XPU compute dies
- HBM memory stacks
- Multiple 6.4T (or 1.6T) SiPho light engines
- High-speed SerDes + die-to-die interfaces

The light engines sit right next to the compute dies. Optical fibers exit the package via detachable connectors, enabling rack-scale or row-scale optical connectivity without copper cables. This delivers:
- 2× bandwidth density vs. 100G solutions
- Dramatically lower power and latency
- Easier thermal management (optics can be placed optimally)

Post-Acquisition Photonic Fabric™

Marvell acquired Celestial AI to accelerate scale-up interconnects. Their PFLink™ (Photonic Fabric Link) chiplet builds on similar CPO principles but targets even higher performance:
- First-gen: 16 Tbps per chiplet
- Uses EAM (electro-absorption) modulators on the PIC + TSMC 4/5nm EIC
- In-package optics + memory semantic FLITs + in-network processing
- Enables multi-rack optical fabrics with <150 ns XPU-to-XPU latency and ~2.5 pJ/bit full link power

Electrical signals leave the host chip (e.g., switch ASIC or XPU): High-speed SerDes (serializer/deserializer) outputs electrical data. 2. Short on-package electrical interconnect to the optical engine (usually via micro-bumps, copper pillars, or redistribution layers on the substrate/interposer). This path is only mm long → minimal power loss and signal degradation. 3. Electronic IC (EIC) in the optical engine: Receives the electrical signal and drives the photonic components (e.g., modulator drivers, transimpedance amplifiers/TIAs). 4. Photonic IC (PIC) does the conversion: - Transmit (Tx): Continuous-wave laser light (usually external, via fiber) enters the PIC. The EIC drives modulators (e.g., micro-ring modulators/MRMs) on the PIC to encode the data onto the light. - Receive (Rx): Incoming light from fiber hits photodetectors on the PIC, which convert it to electrical current. The EIC amplifies and processes it. 5. Fiber coupling: Light enters/exits the PIC via precise couplers (grating couplers with micro-lenses or edge couplers) connected to a fiber array unit (FAU). Fibers then run to the chassis faceplate or other packages. 6. Back to the host chip on the receive side (short electrical path again). The optical engine is typically a 3D-stacked or side-by-side EIC + PIC die (often using hybrid bonding or 2.5D/3D-IC tech). The whole thing sits on an organic substrate or silicon interposer alongside the main ASIC, with external lasers (for reliability/thermal reasons) or sometimes integrated ones. Key enablers: - Silicon photonics (SiPh) on the PIC: Waveguides, MRMs (tiny resonant rings for efficient, compact 200 Gbps+ modulation), germanium photodetectors. - Advanced packaging: 2.5D (interposer), 3D stacking, CoWoS (Chip on Wafer on Substrate), etc. - Fiber attach: Sub-micron precision alignment (challenging but solved with micro-lenses/grating couplers)."}}}, "rest_id": "2055006412573728946", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "217", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2055012160820891651", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "119", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text: @aleabitoreddit What do you think of $FABC they're also in photonics

Will this have huge impact on on $sive? Cpo stock down a lot today due to this news? “TSMC's COUPE (Compact Universal Photonic Engine) is a cutting-edge silicon photonics platform designed for Co-Packaged Optics (CPO), aimed at accelerating chip-to-chip communication in AI data centers. By directly integrating optical engines with high-performance compute silicon (like GPUs or switches) on a 2.5D/3D package, COUPE dramatically reduces power consumption and boosts data bandwidth, addressing the limitations of traditional copper-based interconnects. 

Ansys
 +4
Key Function: COUPE uses SOIC-X to stack an electrical die directly on top of a photonic die, resulting in a compact "engine" that enables ultra-high-density interconnects.
Production Roadmap: TSMC plans to introduce COUPE-based products in 2025 for validation, with mass production scheduled for 2026 integrated into its CoWoS-integrated CPO packages.
Performance Impact: This technology aims to deliver a 2x reduction in power consumption and a 10x reduction in latency compared to existing solutions.
Market Impact: The technology is heavily supported by major industry players like NVIDIA and AMD for their next-generation AI platforms.
Technical Advantages: COUPE features embedded micro-lens arrays, a backside metal reflector, and a grating coupler to minimize optical insertion loss, keeping it below 1.2 dB. 

Design And Reuse
 +6
By moving from pluggable transceivers to on-package CPO using COUPE, data centers can achieve significant power savings, especially in massive AI clusters where data movement is the primary energy cost. “

text:
🚀 5/13 Copper Wall Account Update

Just restructured this account. Ditched the old $5/week into 12 AI/photonics stocks. 

New strategy: $65/week DCA for years into the key constraints & enablers of future compute demand. 

High conviction, speculative, but the upside if these theses play out is massive. Not devastating if they don’t.

Core Thesis: Tech keeps advancing. There will always be new constraints. Get ahead of them.

Thematic Exposure + Picks:

1.  Photonics / Lithography → $EUV (Corgi Semiconductor Photonics ETF)

2.  Memory (the AI bottleneck) → $DRAM (Roundhill Memory ETF)

3.  Space Infrastructure → $MARS (Roundhill Space & Tech ETF)

4.  AI Power & Infrastructure → $AIPO (Defiance AI & Power Infrastructure ETF)

5.  Quantum Computing → $IONQ (direct stock)

This “Copper Wall” is built for long-term resilience in the next waves of tech. Who else is positioning for these themes?

text:
Starting from optics/photonics. How far can I go?

I am an optical/photonics engineer. The reason I started PhotonCap was simple. I wanted to see how the lasers, optical fibers, and transceivers I have worked with every day get priced in the stock market. When someone who knows the defect density of an InP epi wafer looks at the stock price of the company that makes it, sometimes you can see things the market is missing. That was the starting point.

The glass substrate investment map was optics. CPO transceivers, SiPh, InP lasers. Right in the center of what I do. The LEO satellite map was a step further out. The optical component inside an OISL terminal is my territory, but orbital mechanics and launch vehicle economics are not. The subsea cable map went further still. The III-V pump laser inside an EDFA repeater is my territory, but the cable-laying vessel market and national security framing are not.

To be honest, there are limits to interpreting fields outside my expertise from a perspective that is also outside my expertise. I can find the number that says 60 cable-laying vessels exist worldwide, but someone in the shipbuilding industry understands the operating economics of a single vessel better than I do. I can back-calculate that NEC’s subsea revenue is in the single-digit percentage range, but an analyst covering Japanese IT conglomerates will do a better job valuing that business unit than I can.

Sometimes subscribers reach out. “Analyze XX purely from XX perspective.” When I get these requests, it weighs on my conscience. Is it right to publish analysis, behind a paywall, on a topic where I am not certain whether my interpretation is correct or not?

So I thought about it seriously.

Here is what I can and cannot do. Explaining why a material is a bottleneck at the optical component tier, why a process is difficult, why a qualification takes years. That is something I can do. Macro, geopolitics, valuation models, shipbuilding, power infrastructure. These are areas where I have no hands-on engineering experience. In these areas, I am a researcher reading and cross-checking public sources, not a practitioner with field experience.

Until now, I have mostly interpreted things through an optics lens. Going forward, I want to widen the aperture slightly, expanding from optics into a broader engineering perspective. Not just photonics, but compound semiconductor manufacturing, packaging, system integration, reliability testing. A wider engineering layer for looking at supply chains, while being clear about which layers I have touched firsthand and which I have not.

What changes is the scope. What does not change is the principle. Primary sources first. Limits of inference stated explicitly. When I do not know, I say I do not know. That stays the same.

I am continuing to think about the topics you have been requesting. Within the range I can cover, I will write as honestly as I can.

Best Regards,
PhotonCap

text:
The US Photonics Supply Chain: The Unsung Hero Powering AI’s Explosive Growth While everyone focuses on GPUs, the real bottleneck — and opportunity — lies in the optical interconnects that move massive amounts of data at light speed inside AI data centers. Gemini Trading’s infographic maps the complete US Photonics Supply Chain for AI infrastructure:  Key Layers:InP Substrates → AXT (global leader)
Active Optical Chips → Lumentum, Coherent, Broadcom, MACOM
TOSA/ROSA & Optical Engines → Core components for high-speed transmission
Optical Modules & AOC → AAOI, Ciena, Fabrinet
Networking Systems → Arista, Broadcom, NVIDIA
Terminal AI Applications → NVIDIA GB200 (paired with 18x 800G optical modules), Quantum-X CPO

Market Data:
Global Optical Module Market expected to grow from US$51 billion in 2024 to US$89 billion in 2029 at a 12.5% CAGR. DSP components alone make up 30-40% of an 800G module’s BOM. Why it matters:
AI training clusters are scaling to millions of GPUs. Traditional copper can’t keep up — photonics (light-based data transfer) is the only solution for low-latency, high-bandwidth, energy-efficient connectivity at scale. 2025–2026 will see large-scale deployment of 1.6T systems. Investment angle:
Companies across the photonics stack — especially those enabling 800G/1.6T modules and co-packaged optics (CPO) — stand to benefit enormously as AI capex continues surging. The photonic supply chain is still under-the-radar compared to GPU hype, but it may deliver some of the strongest multi-year growth in the entire AI ecosystem.(Not financial advice — always DYOR and manage risk appropriately.)@GeminiTrading

text:
Nvidia isn’t just selling the picks and shovels — it’s quietly buying the entire gold mine.
By dropping billions into optics, power, photonics, and AI cloud players, Jensen is locking down the physical bottlenecks that could slow AI scaling. This isn’t random VC money; it’s vertical control that funnels more demand straight back to Nvidia GPUs while building a deeper moat.
Classic platform power move: own the dependencies. Expect the flywheel to spin harder.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

embedded_text:
From what I have read about $PENG we have the following:

→ CXL lets servers tap a shared pool of DRAM over a fast interconnect. 
→ KV cache is the running memory LLMs need during inference, it overflows GPU VRAM fast.

It's not going away in 3 years, which is why $LITE premiums keep going higher since they're backlogged into 2028. $SIVE supplies CW lasers and is highly tethered to CPO and now pluggable transcivers for 1.6T and 3.2... For expected companies like $JBL, Ayar, Lightmatter, Lightelligence, $POET, $MRVL Celestial, and $AMD. This isn't a "trade", it's the core chokepoint and IP holder for the next generation of photonics. And it's a comfortable hold for the next few years as they scale to become the next $LITE. The risk I personally see (since they're already qualified with so many players), it's mainly how much TAM they can capture of the overall optical supercycle. (And potential risks with Win Semi volume ramp, but Win is massive so I can sleep tightly there). As just supplying lasers isn't enough to justify valuation. It's TAM expansion downward into making the entire ELS or entire pluggable transceiver that makes these laser companies so valuable. Then afterward, they can vertically integrating upward for gross margin expansion upward like $COHR into doing the laser fabs or even substrate level. And that in my view is a very asymmetric risk/reward ratio as we've already seen this done with $LITE as they went from $2B to $80B. 2. $LPK - Is the purest exposure, without the messy financials of SKC Absolics, as the next advanced packaging shift for glass substrates. Almost every single major semi company from $INTC to Samsung are adopting glass substrates. $LPK is basically $ASML of this chokepoint, since they supply to ~80% of the global players currently. Yes, there's "trade cycles" for equipment suppliers like $ASML, where if there's more foundry capex, ASML scales up. But if there's downturns, these tend to perform poorly, and don't capture all the volume ramp that happens after. However, if the MC is $650m and they're making $100-200M, revenue per costumer volume ramped, the amount they make from the glass substrate cycle will likely exceed current valuations. And they'll have baseline fundamentals (as more companies adopt the packaging shift), that keeps their valuation up. It's just a waiting game for volume ramp at this point. 3. $AAOI - This is literally $INTC but for America + Photonics. It's like saying Intel is not a long term investment. Guess where all your optical transcivers are made? China. Thailand. Malaysia. If you look at Innolight, Eoptolink, $FN, and others. AOI is building the largest Made in America supply chains for both CW laser fab, as well as 800g, 1.6T assembly. Yes, there are pluggable cycle ups and downs to this as well. There's going to be a wave for 1.6T next year, then CPO cannibalizes pluggables down the road. But since they make the entire supply chain in house, they have extreme optionality for other segments. And like $NVDA older gen-GPUs, there's going to be sovereign DC requirements for older gen pluggables from names like $AAOI. It's likely going to keep rising as it hits that $400m+/month revenue target H2 2026. There's just a lot of different short term volatility along the way like the $600m dilution. 4. $IQE - ??? It's one of the most important players in the Western word for epiwafers. $MTSI went out of their way to pay off IQE's debt because they can't have them going under. $IQE is also supplying to $LITE. The world is currently bottlenecked both on the epiwafer level from Landmark comments and InP substrate levels. Their financials were track but the raw book value, and value they hold to the entire Western supply chain... completely justifies their valuation. And other optical companies will not let their core upstream supply chain go under. As these tens of millions worth of materials would screw up tens of billions worth of downstream products. Again photonics is the next generation architecture required to scale AI. It's not Quantum where it's just "In development". It's literally here and the architecture of choice by $NVDA. I would not be surprised if all of these are a lot higher in 3-4 years time. People who think it's one and done in 3 months time "only because I mentioned it" don't know what they're talking about. Institutions would have bought up the name eventually (like Point 72 on $IQE) and retail would only find out after their valuations are 600% higher. Should really do the research before adding comments like these: These are all forward growth companies that require in-depth supply chain knowledge."}}}, "quotedRefResult": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "rest_id": "2055299648093340047"}}, "rest_id": "2055822766600016238", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "589046", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2056079647406747852", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "5179", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
When cars were invented, people didn’t stop riding horses overnight.

The better technology eventually won. But the transition took longer - and usually messier - than we expected.

#Photonics in #AI networking may be similar.

Optics IS the next major upgrade:
- lower power
- faster speeds
- better scaling for massive AI clusters

But copper still works well enough today:
- cheaper
- simpler
- still heavily used in many current AI clusters

That’s why #hyperscalers are still building large #copper-heavy systems today.

Photonics today looks more like an upgrade cycle than a hard #bottleneck like #EUV or advanced chip manufacturing.

The technology is improving quickly.

But the industry still needs time on:
- packaging
- reliability
- integration at scale
- deployment economics

We’ve seen similar setups before: #solar, #EV charging, fiber buildouts, early #5G.

The direction was right. 
The rollout was slower. 
Valuations reset hard.

 #photonics #optics #CPO #siliconphotonics will no doubt  become the “standard,” but..

IF the rollout takes longer too, 

how much of today’s valuation still holds up?

As investors we all want to believe we’ve found the next “certainty.”

Reality is rarely that clean.

Still worth thinking about.

"to_index": 2222}]}, "text": "There’s a lot to digest from the last week with $SIVE. The $JBL 1.6T collaboration, together with the confirmed CW laser shortage and partnerships with O-Net and Enablence Technologies, feels like a decisive step forward. In the Annual Report from May 13, $SIVE directly addresses “discussions with hyperscalers and pluggable transceiver suppliers” that point to a structural shortage of CW lasers. This is happening at exactly the right time, AI training and inference clusters are pushing explosive demand for higher bandwidth, lower power and denser interconnects. What really stands out to me is how the dots are connecting. $JBL, key partner to $AMZN, $MSFT and $GOOGL has chosen $SIVE’s high-performance InP DFB lasers for 1.6T pluggable modules. At the same time, Amazon’s recent private placement with AIChip (Ayar Labs’ lead customer) signals major design wins ahead. And $SIVE is the sole supplier of the critical laser arrays to Ayar Labs. The same pattern keeps repeating. O-Net Technologies (with strong North American and Asian hyperscaler ties) and Enablence are developing ELS modules with $SIVE lasers for CPO. POET Technologies $POET just secured a $50 million initial order from Lumilens. Everywhere the big players are moving, $SIVE seems to be part of the picture. On the manufacturing side, the WIN Semiconductors partnership together with the own Glasgow fab gives $SIVE real independence and scalable capacity (30k–35k wafers/year) in a very tight InP market. All of this is coming together right now. Pluggables give near-term volume, while CPO offers the big efficiency jump ahead. The photonics TAM is exploding from $14B to $154B in just two years and $SIVE is positioned right in the middle of it. With this level of validation across the value chain, I believe we are likely to see more partnerships and firm purchase orders soon. For $SIVE, this feels like a real inflection point. The company is transitioning from deep-tech innovator to a strategically critical, independent supplier in the optical backbone of next-generation AI. The hyperscalers keep choosing $SIVE for a reason. Execution is there, the connections are deepening, and this momentum feels increasingly unstoppable."}}}, "rest_id": "2056055516107866286", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "196630", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2056067956526760180", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "2592", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Graduate students advancing the future of photonics, optics, lasers, and electro-optics are encouraged to apply for the IEEE Photonics Society Graduate Student Scholarship Program before the 30 May 2026 deadline.

Up to $10,000 in scholarships will be awarded to outstanding IEEE Photonics Society Student Members based on research excellence and contributions to Society publications and conferences.

Awards will also include:
• Certificate of Achievement
• Complimentary registration to the IEEE Photonics Conference 2026
• Recognition at IPC 2026 in Denver, Colorado

The program supports graduate students across the global photonics community, with awards distributed across:
• Americas (Regions 1–7 & 9)
• Europe/Middle East/Africa (Region 📷
• Asia/Pacific (Region 10)

Application Deadline: 30 May 2026

Apply now and learn more about the scholarship program and recent honorees through the IEEE Photonics Society: https://t.co/qrnNoBss0f

text:
Spent the weekend reading $CRDO order book commentary.

The phrase that stuck: capacity sold out through 2027.

Silicon Photonics & CPO (#2 this week) is the wave: the whole AI server stack is mid-migration from copper to light, 800G to 1.6T transceivers, every hyperscaler ordering at once.

$CRDO owns the connection layer that bridges the switch to the optical link, the on-ramp every fabric has to cross.

Picks-and-shovels of the picks-and-shovels.

$32Bn cap, 30x EV/Sales (price tag vs yearly revenue, like paying 30 years of a store's sales to own it).

Rich on the screen, but the book is locked through 2027, and that's a math problem the bears haven't solved.

Price is starting to move, fundamentals say sold-out.

I'm long, adding on chop, sizing for the multi-year ride not the next print.

The why is more important than the when.

Because the when always catches up to the why.

text:
I interviewed @bubbleboi about his ratings of AI supply chain bottlenecks.

We talked about DRAM, advanced packaging, CPO, HBF, PCBs, power delivery, etc. 

0:00 HBM, DRAM, the cartel
7:24 Silicon photonics, CPO, Lumentum lasers
11:35 Advanced packaging: TSMC vs Intel
13:49 HBF: Sandisk/SK monopoly window
17:02 Memory accelerators + TurboQuant
22:35 PCBs + Unitika
27:27 Power: transition from 48V to 800V
31:32 Outsourced assembly + test, fiber coupling
36:23 HBM vs DRAM vs NAND in 2-3 years
42:03 Where will hardware founders come from?
44:10 Alt accelerators: Etched, Taalas, MatX
48:43 Emerging tech: CPO bearishness
49:23 Hyperclouds
49:53 HBF timeline + CXL
51:48 Voltage + cooling wall
56:30 Rapid fire: Intel, Nvidia, TSMC, Alphabet
1:05:25 ASML, Hynix, Lumentum, Wolfspeed
1:13:25 Building conviction in Intel
1:19:37 Pitching Intel to funds
1:22:39 X accounts, analysts + why care about any of this
